cards.idea: The poem is Philip Larkin's This Be The Verse (1971). Beneath its blunt, profane opening, its real argument is gentler: people are wounded by parents who were themselves wounded — an inherited cycle — with an unspoken plea to stop passing it on.
cards.why: It names the episode's whole engine. The point isn't to blame parents but to break the chain. Watch how it reframes both Ted's grief and Jamie's father wound that follow.
cards.source: Philip Larkin, This Be The Verse (1971); recaps in ScreenRant, Collider, and Looper.
cards.groundingNotes: Three recaps confirm Mae recites Larkin's 'This Be The Verse' in S3E11; Larkin's 1971 authorship is well documented.

cards.title: Ted confronts his mother's cheerfulness
cards.moment: Ted thanks his mother for raising him, then tells her plainly that her relentless 'everything's fine' brightness after his father's death taught him to bottle his own grief — and to hide it when he wasn't okay. She owns it, and they reconcile.
cards.idea: Unprocessed grief gets handed down as a coping style. The 'performed okayness' Ted learned from his mom is the same habit that later fed his panic attacks. Naming it is how he starts to break it.
cards.why: The emotional climax of the hour, and the payoff to Ted's long, hard-won openness to therapy. (Context: his father's death is established back in Season 2 — the card keeps the focus on the coping pattern, not the loss itself.)
cards.source: Recaps at TheCinemaholic and TV Tropes.
cards.groundingNotes: TheCinemaholic and TV Tropes confirm Ted calls out Dottie's performed cheerfulness after his father's death and that they reconcile. Father's suicide is S2 backstory — series-scope spoiler.

cards.title: Jamie's bravado comes from his father
cards.moment: Back in his hometown to face Man City, the team's brashest player quietly admits his whole drive was about proving his estranged father wrong — and that the man's absence unsettles him more than his presence ever did.
cards.idea: Cockiness can be armor built around a parental wound. Jamie's mother tells him he doesn't need to prove anything to his dad anymore; letting that go is what frees him to play.
cards.why: It reframes three seasons of Jamie's swagger in a single confession, and rhymes with Ted's own parent reckoning happening in parallel. (The locker-room punch that estranged them is Season 2, not here.)
cards.source: Recaps at TV Tropes, Nerds & Beyond, and The Review Geek.
cards.groundingNotes: Recaps confirm Jamie's homecoming breakdown and father wound in E11; the estranging punch is S2's 'Man City', kept distinct.

cards.tag: Where the soft Jamie came from
cards.title: Jamie's loving mum and stepdad
cards.moment: A trip to Jamie's childhood home on his old council estate reveals a doting mother and a kind stepfather, quietly explaining where the softer, generous Jamie underneath the swagger came from.
cards.idea: The warmth was always there — just on his mother's side. The contrast with his abusive biological father reframes Jamie: his hunger to prove himself came from one parent, his decency from another.
cards.why: Easy-to-miss texture that makes Jamie's whole arc click. His stepdad is named Simon; his mum is the source of the kindness the show finally lets surface.
cards.source: Recaps at Nerds & Beyond and The Spoilist.
cards.groundingNotes: Recaps confirm the council-estate visit to Jamie's warm mum and stepdad Simon, contrasting his abusive father.

cards.title: 'Home' from The Wiz over the credits
cards.moment: As the hour closes on the pull of home, a soaring cover of 'Home' — the showstopper from the musical The Wiz — plays over the credits, extending the show's long thread of Wizard-of-Oz nods about leaving and returning.
cards.idea: The needle-drop (Brandi Carlile's cover) ties the closing beat to Dorothy's longing to go home. Some viewers also note the names — Ted's mom is Dottie, short for Dorothy; his son is Henry, the name of Dorothy's uncle in Oz — though that symbolism is a fan reading, not a stated intent.
cards.why: A quiet aesthetic choice doing emotional heavy lifting: the song tells you where Ted is headed before he says it.
cards.source: Stereogum and Rolling Stone on the Carlile cover; name symbolism is a noted fan reading.
cards.groundingNotes: The 'Home' (The Wiz) credits cue in E11 is well sourced; the Dottie/Dorothy + Henry name symbolism is recapper commentary, framed as a reading.

cards.title: 'Your son misses you'
cards.moment: Ted's mother finally admits the real reason she crossed an ocean: to tell him his boy misses him. Ted admits he misses Henry too, but fears losing him. The hour closes on Ted resolved to say something to Rebecca.
cards.idea: The reconciliation with his mom and this small admission shift something in Ted. Once he stops performing okayness, he can feel how far he is from home — and start moving toward it.
cards.why: The turn that sets up the show's ending. Watch how the parent reckoning and the 'Home' song work together to point Ted across the ocean. (It gestures at the finale, so it carries series-level weight.)
cards.source: Recaps at TheCinemaholic and The Hollywood Reporter.
cards.groundingNotes: TheCinemaholic and HR confirm Dottie's 'your son misses you', Ted's fear of losing Henry, and the closing cliffhanger. Series-scope spoiler.

cards.title: Beard forgives Nate
cards.moment: After Nate keeps refusing to come back to the club, Coach Beard seeks him out, makes peace, and personally asks him to return.
cards.idea: Nate's redemption advances not because he earns his way back but because someone he wronged chooses to forgive him — Beard, who was once forgiven himself. It folds the episode's parents-and-belonging theme into the idea of returning to a found family.
cards.why: A quiet counterpoint to the Ted and Jamie arcs: forgiveness here is something offered, not demanded. (Whether it comes too easily is a fair question critics raised.)
cards.source: Recaps at TV Tropes and ComicBook.
cards.groundingNotes: TV Tropes and ComicBook confirm Beard forgives Nate and invites him back after Jade gets him fired from Taste of Athens.

cards.title: Dottie earned an Emmy nod
cards.moment: Ted's mother is played by a veteran character actor whose single-episode appearance was singled out at the Emmys.
cards.idea: Dottie is played by Becky Ann Baker (the mom in Freaks and Geeks and Girls). Her one-episode turn here drew a 2023 Emmy nomination for Outstanding Guest Actress in a Comedy Series — a measure of how much weight this single visit carries.
cards.why: Worth knowing how much craft went into a character who appears for just one hour and reshapes Ted's whole backstory.
cards.source: Television Academy 2023 nominees list; profile at GoldDerby.
cards.groundingNotes: Television Academy and GoldDerby confirm a 2023 nomination for Outstanding Guest Actress in a Comedy Series for the Dottie role.

cards.title: Richmond finally beats Man City 2-0
cards.moment: After years of humiliating losses to Manchester City, Richmond wins 2-0 on the day — and the player once defined by selfishness helps make the opener and scores the clincher in the city of his estranged father.
cards.idea: The win pays off Jamie's whole arc from selfish star to generous teammate, set up by Roy's season-long mentorship. The result matters less than who Jamie has become to earn it.
cards.why: A long-running rivalry finally turns, and it turns because of the change in one character — in the one place that change hurts most.
cards.source: Recaps at First Curiosity and TV Tropes.
cards.groundingNotes: Two recaps confirm the 2-0 win, Colin scoring off a Jamie assist and Jamie's late goal.

cards.title: 'Fought & Lost' debuts here
cards.moment: An anthemic original rock ballad scores a key emotional stretch of the penultimate episode.
cards.idea: 'Fought & Lost' was written for the show — performed by Sam Ryder featuring Queen's Brian May, co-written by Ryder with series composer Tom Howe and Jamie Hartman. It released as a single on the same day the episode aired, 24 May 2023.
cards.why: A small window into how the show builds feeling with custom music rather than only borrowed tracks — right down to recruiting a legendary guitarist.
cards.source: Wikipedia ('Fought & Lost') and Film Music Reporter.
cards.groundingNotes: Confirmed via Wikipedia and Film Music Reporter: Ryder/Howe/Hartman song featuring Brian May, debuts in 'Mom City', single out 24 May 2023.

cards.title: 'Mom City' and 'Man City'
cards.moment: The episode about two men and their mothers is set around a match against Manchester City — and its title plays the two against each other.
cards.idea: Richmond travels to face Manchester City ('Man City'), while the hour centers on mothers — Ted's mom Dottie and Jamie's mum. Viewers read the title as a deliberate riff on the earlier Season 2 episode 'Man City,' swapping in 'Mom.' (That reading is recapper interpretation, not a stated creator intent.)
cards.why: A neat bit of wordplay that signals the episode's whole double focus before it starts.
cards.source: The Hollywood Reporter and Wikipedia; the pun reading is fan/recap interpretation.
cards.groundingNotes: Fixture and dual-mothers focus firmly sourced; the 'Mom City'/'Man City' pun is recapper interpretation, framed as such.

cards.title: Ed Sheeran wrote a song just for the finale
cards.moment: Part of the farewell is scored by a brand-new song written for the show, rather than an existing track pulled off a record.
cards.idea: Ed Sheeran wrote and performed 'A Beautiful Game' (co-written with Foy Vance and Max Martin) specifically for this episode. It was released as a single the same day the finale aired, and later won a Creative Arts Emmy for Outstanding Original Music and Lyrics.
cards.why: A measure of how much care went into the goodbye: the show commissioned bespoke music to close it out. Worth a listen on its own after the episode.
cards.source: Billboard and Deadline reporting; 2023 Creative Arts Emmy for Outstanding Original Music and Lyrics.
cards.groundingNotes: Confirmed: Sheeran original co-written with Foy Vance and Max Martin, released finale day, plays over Beard's video, later an Emmy winner.

cards.title: The team's musical surprise was real
cards.moment: The squad's goodbye performance lands as a genuine surprise because it was staged to actually surprise the lead actor.
cards.idea: Director Declan Lowney has said the cast rehearsed the number separately so Jason Sudeikis hadn't seen it before the camera rolled, making the emotion on screen real. A drone was brought in to capture the celebration from above.
cards.why: Knowing the reaction is unrehearsed changes how the scene plays: you're watching a real send-off, not a performance of one. Watch the lead's face the first time the song starts.
cards.source: Director Declan Lowney, interviews with CinemaBlend and Awards Radar.
cards.groundingNotes: From Lowney interviews (essentially single-source); cast rehearsed separately so the star's reaction was genuine, captured with a drone. Self-contained production fact.

cards.title: The title comes from The Sound of Music
cards.moment: At his final training session, the squad ambushes the coach with a full performance of a children's goodbye number from a classic musical he adores, turning a routine into a send-off.
cards.idea: The episode is named for 'So Long, Farewell,' the von Trapp children's goodnight song from Rodgers & Hammerstein's The Sound of Music. The title does double duty: a farewell to the coach inside the story, and a farewell to the series.
cards.why: A small key to the coach's sentimental streak: his love of the old musical becomes the team's way of saying goodbye. Listen for the children's-song structure under the football setting.
cards.source: So Long, Farewell from The Sound of Music (Rodgers & Hammerstein, 1959 musical / 1965 film); SlashFilm and NPR finale recaps.
cards.groundingNotes: Confirmed: episode title is the von Trapp goodbye song, performed by the squad as a send-off; a self-contained reference that does not spoil endings.

cards.title: Roy asks if people can really change
cards.moment: The gruff loner asks to join the support circle, admits he's struggling to change, and is told no one ever reaches perfect — you just keep asking for help and keep moving toward better.
cards.idea: The episode's plain wisdom: change isn't arriving at perfection, it's the ongoing willingness to ask for and accept help. (The Aristotelian 'habit of virtue' framing is an interpretive gloss, not named on screen.)
cards.why: It states the show's progress-over-perfection ethic outright: change is the ongoing willingness to ask for help, never an arrival at perfect.
cards.source: TV Tropes recap; Today finale explainer. Aristotle link is interpretive.
cards.groundingNotes: Confirmed: Roy asks to join the Diamond Dogs and the group debates whether people can really change and chase perfection. The Aristotle / habit-of-virtue links are factsheet framing, not named on screen, kept as a gloss. Series-scope payoff.
